cards.text:
0.3 to 1.5%.

That is the KSure trade credit insurance premium — and the Korean government covers 90% of any loss if your buyer defaults.

For most overseas buyers, the biggest fear on a first K-beauty deal is not the product. It is payment. The seller worries about non-payment. The buyer worries about prepaying and getting nothing. Both fears compound when neither side has worked together before.

We see four tools that resolve almost every variant of this fear:

1. LC (Letter of Credit) — safest, settlement 30 to 60 days, issuance fee 0.1 to 0.3%. Standard for large or first-time deals where neither side wants to extend credit blind.

2. TT (Telegraphic Transfer) — fastest, used in over 70% of K-beauty B2B deals. Typical structure: 30 to 50% deposit, balance after the B/L copy is sent.

3. KSure trade credit insurance — Korean government covers 90% of loss if a buyer defaults. Premium 0.3 to 1.5% of invoice. For a first deal, this is the structural safety net that lets a Korean seller accept TT 30/70 terms.

4. FX hedge — KRW/USD 6-month volatility runs 5 to 10%. A standard forward contract costs 0.5 to 1% per year and removes both upside and downside. On a first deal, hedge cost is cheap insurance against a 5–10% swing.

Recommended combination for a first K-beauty order: FOB Busan + TT 30/70 + KSure + forward hedge at PO signing. Each piece does one job. Together they cover the four fears.

Fraud check: verify the business registration certificate, and only deal with suppliers KSure will register. KSure eligibility is itself a compliance signal.

Which of these four did you not know about before your first Korean import deal? We are curious.

#KBeauty #B2BSourcing #TradeFinance #LetterOfCredit #KSure
